sentences.text: Arie Van Mansum.
A-R-I-E, capital V-A-N, capital M-A-N-S-U-M.
No, that's the name when I was born.
That's correct.
Yes, in Canada, they use me-- they call me, usually, Harry.
H-A-R-R-Y.
During the wartime, I used the name Harry Van Desande.
V-A-N D-E-S-A-N-D-E.
To fool the Germans.
And to-- and if people don't know my name, maybe, [INAUDIBLE].
Nothing.
Nothing obvious.
I was born in the city of Utrecht, spelled U-T-R-E-C-H-T, in Holland.
I stayed in Utrecht till age six.
And then my dad was transferred to Maastricht.
And the rest of my life, I used to live in Maastricht.
M-A-A-S-T-R-I-C-H-T.
That's also in Holland, correct, yeah.
The southern part.
It been province of Limburg.
L-I-M-B-U-R-G.
March the 5th, 1920.
I am now 76 years old.
My father's name was Gerrit.
G-E-R-R-I-T.
Yes.
My father was an laborer and later on-- later on a, a conductor with the Dutch railroad.
And he also had an part-time job as a custodian of the church.
No.
He was just a man of the church, and that was about it.
There was not much organization there in Limburg at that time.
He, he--
--was-- yes, he was an handyman.
He made toys for the kids, and fixed the house, and whatever.
What had to be  done, he did.
My mother's name was Neeltje, N-E-E-L-T-J-E.
My mother was a housewife, she-- a, a dedicated housewife.
She looked well after the family and was also, as my father,   an example for the family.
I had one brother and two sisters.
My brother, he emigrated after the war to Australia, where he died about 30 years ago.
My two sisters, both were-- both were unmarried.
They lived in Holland.
And both died some couple of years ago.
No longer alive, that's right.
Well, my brother, he-- during the wartime, he had to go into hiding for the Germans because he wanted to work in, in Germany.
But he was arrested and was, for a while, in a prison in Holland.
And they let him go because of his heart condition.
And after the war, he joined the Navy, got  his training in England.
And then he met his, his, his, his present wife, his wife that's still alive.
And he married [? her ?] in Holland.
And they moved, both, to Australia.
My sisters, the oldest one, she was a nurse in Amsterdam in a hospital there.
And she lived, for the most of the life, either in, in Amsterdam, or in a city close to Amsterdam named Leiden, L-E-I-D-E-N.
My youngest sister always was in Maastricht.
And she became an teacher in home economics.
And she lived with my parents until after their death.
Maastricht was a very old city.
And it had buildings that-- churches built in 1200.
It's a very impressive city.
It was-- however, of course, because we were an, an Protestant minority in Maastricht, we were not well-liked by the people in Maastricht due for our religion.
And so we always had a close-knit, close family, together, and members from the church.
But it was-- country it is now-- it was very difficult, at that time, to live there as a non-Catholic.
99.9% was Roman Catholic there, yes.
My-- as I told  you, my father was with the Dutch Railroad.
And when he was transferred to Maastricht, he bought to-- in a common home in a-- in co-op.
That was fine.
It was with the Dutch railroad.
And it was row houses, small row houses.
And the majority for the people who lived there were also working with the railroad company.
And in that neighborhood, I grew up.
Around the age of 10, most  of my friends  were people, all of them in Protestant school I went to, or from our church.
Yes, I first went to an public elementary school.
And then I went to an Protestant high school until I graduated.
Well, my favorite subjects were math, algebra, I-- that, that kind of stuff.
I'm not-- I was not that keen on-- in language, though.
It was a friendly place.
It was not-- it was not strict at all.
And we could-- we could pick some subjects, yes, ourselves.
Not necessarily close.
You know, we had many friends.
But I-- we went-- people were there with them, you know.
Not-- some people you were closer to, but not really close.
We had that there.
That's right, yes.
Yeah.
No.
He-- for financial reasons, he became a custodian of the church.
And he, with my mom, he cleaned the church.
And he opened the door on a Sunday and opened up the doors when there were meetings.
And he maintained the, the garden around the church.
Oh, yes, yeah.
Yeah.
He spent lots of time with us.
I still remember that he bought a second-hand steam train, train.
And he played with that.
And he let  us play with it.
But we usually we were-- we looked at it, how he did it-- does that himself.
We watched him, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
It was scared with the steam that came out, you know.
But he enjoyed it, yeah, yeah, yeah.
Yes, my family was religious from upbringing.
My, my, my parents, we all were members of the Reformed Church, a small church in Maastricht.

They practiced their religion by going twice to church on Sunday and by-- at home, you know, educating us, us in the Bible, Old and New Testament, and reading the Bible, and praying on the table, yeah.
Well, we, we read quite a bit out of the Old and New Testament, yes--
--on the-- on the table, when we have our sitting together.
Most, most, most was read by  my parents, yes.
Well, the moral lesson we got from the readings, and also from the teaching of our parents, was to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look after the poor, look after the people who are homeless, and so on.
And they were an example to that.
And we, we followed that  later on.
That started very early in  my life, yes.
It was not before the war because there was practically no racism.
And as a matter of fact, we didn't knew-- didn't even know who was a Jew or not.
But after the-- 1932, when we had an influx of Jewish immigrants from Germany, then we became aware at what racism was and what hate was.
Well, the only data we, we got was to listen to radio  and the papers what happens in Germany.
And that was an eye-opener for us, what racism really was.
The persecution of the Jews, the way that they had to flee-- leave the country, and came to, to Holland and other countries, maybe.
And, and there was quite an influx, yeah.
No, not at all.
Well, we-- our city hall set up a committee when I was 19 years of age to look after those people.
And they asked me if-- our church asked me to be a representative there.
And that committee  had regular meetings.
And we discussed what-- how to handle the influx and what to do with those people.
It was upsetting, of course.
But the real death came after Hitler came into Holland, yeah.
19 years.
And--
And I was one, one of the members of that committee to look after the people who came from Germany and who were-- looked-- who were looking for places to, to stay, and whatever, yeah.
No.
This was formed with the city of [NON-ENGLISH]-- the city of Maastricht.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Not that I'm aware of, no.
No, no.
We had-- we hadn't ever had a problem, no.
The only way I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] know about it is, of course, that people always talk about-- that's, that's a Jew.
And I found that-- I found that quite disturbing.
Well, if, if, if, if, for example, somebody bought an-- a, a shirt in a store, if it was done in the store   of Mrs.
Johnson, they said, I went-- he, he, he, he-- they treated me not well.
But if it was a Jew, they said, the Jew got me again, selling me a bad shirt.
And that is, in my eyes, is a form of racism.
In the press, you know, in, in the press and by radio, in-- well, when I was 13, 14 years old.
Well, we, we heard his yelling on the radio all the time, you know, if, if he was [INAUDIBLE] speaking engagement somewhere.
And we heard about his stand on, on Jews and other minority groups in, in our papers and also in our radio broadcast.
He was very anti-Semitic.
He-- as a matter of fact, you know, and it was all known in Holland that he was planning to kill the Jews.
No.
We, we only became aware of that when they occupied Holland.
And they make propaganda with showing the, the, the movie and things like that.
And they had big billboards with all kinds of slogans against the Jews, Nazi [INAUDIBLE] mind, yeah.
Oh, yeah, of course.
We were concerned about happened to the Jews and also what's going to happen to all of us, you know.
Well, we have-- we all had fears that he would enter Holland, you know, and occupy Holland, and that he would do the same thing in Holland as that he did  in Germany.
We were-- had not close connections with our neighbors, really.
Later on, of course, when the occupation came, they showed to be also Nazi-friendly, yeah.
Nazism was, according to myself, and according to the teachings in our church, was anti-Christian.

And as a matter of fact, in, in-- our church had a declaration announcing it, that anybody who became a member of the Nazi party was-- had to be dismissed and couldn't longer be a  member of the church.
That did actually happen, yes.
Well, most of them, they resigned beforehand, because, you know, this-- that's what happens, usually.
Not that many, not that many.
But it was a small, little [? bit, ?] yeah.
No.
No.
None at all.
No.
No, not at all.
No.
There wasn't-- no, there was  not even a problem, yeah.
The Jews who lived in Holland were very-- still felt at home.

And I still remember that if there was an lottery for the-- for the Jewish cause, that our prime minister would-- on the-- on-- by radio, of course, ask the people to buy this for, for, for that-- for that cause, buy the lottery tickets.
I still remember our queen, Queen Wilhelmina at the time, had a special seat in the synagogue in Amsterdam.
And for occasions, she attended the synagogues.
She really was well [? in there. ?]
Yeah.
Well, the majority didn't know whether you were or not, because we went along with each other just like anybody else.
And when the Germans, here, asked people to have an star on their-- on, on their jacket, or whatever-- then all of a sudden, hey, is that guy Jewish?
I didn't know that.
And so, so, so, so [? it happened. ?] There was no problem there.
Not much.
No, we hardly talked to them.
No.
In our neighborhood--
--never did any Jew live there at all.
Not-- after the invasion, of course, they, well, had to print stuff that was handed over to them, by the Nazis.
Before, they were-- had no-- everybody was comfortable with the, the Jews in Holland.
And we had no problem with them.
Forced to, yeah.
They took the editors away, and put the Nazi editors instead of that.
And they make this an-- a propaganda tool.
And as result, we all-- we kind of cancelled the subscription to the papers.
No, never.
Well, I was, at that time, an, an a patient in the hospital.
And we, more or less, expected he was going to do something like that, even if Holland were neutral.
And it was a surprise, of course, on May the 10th, all of a sudden, we was-- woke up in the morning, and there were hundreds of planes in the air.
And we had-- and, well, it was a shock.
I was in the hospital, at that time, yeah.
I had a kidney ailment, yeah.
My family was living in Maastricht, yes.
All lived in-- at their home-- at their  home in Maastricht, yeah.
Not right away.
Not right away.
The-- we were, at first, scared.
And then later on, they-- everything went back to normal.
And we, more or less, believed that there was nothing going on right away.
That came on later, yeah.
Well, the streets were, of course, filled with German soldiers.
Wherever you went, you know, there was soldiers walking around in uniform.
And also, some pro-Nazi Dutch people went on the street in their uniform.
And they had their own thing, set up their own party, the NSB.
And, and they had, had the same greetings as the Germans did, the Nazis did.
So that was that.
That's right.
That's right, yes.
Yeah.
Not much except, of course, that, that we had to use food stamps for food.
And there was-- less and less food we got, and bread, and meat, and butter, and that kind of stuff.
There were--
--rations yeah, right.
Yeah, yeah.
Yes, later on, they had a curfew.
You were not allowed to go on the street at-- after 8 o'clock.
Yeah, until early in the morning.
That's right, yeah.
There was-- there was plenty of work.
It was-- lots and lots of boys, aged 18 and 19 were taken away and brought to Germany for-- to work for the army.
So-- and there were quite a few.
Then, of course, the, the empty places had  to filled with unemployed people.
So that way there was full employment going  on, yeah.
Lots of new laws came into effect, in a way, yeah, set up by Seyss-Inquart, who was the, the man who governed Holland on behalf of Hitler.
Seyss-Inquart.
Oh, you ask me to do this?
[LAUGHTER]
Austria.
He was an Austrian, yes.
Well, there was a law of gatherings.
We were not allowed to have big gatherings anymore.
The, the curfew, of course, was one of the laws.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a whole bunch of new, new laws you had to, to, to try to-- and we were-- we had to hand in our radios, you know.
We're not allowed to listen to the broadcasts from either England or America.
So we only listen-- were allowed to listen to the German broadcast.
Well, they were afraid we were going to read-- going to hear the truth of what was really going to happen.
So we were only just allowed to listen to German propaganda.
That's what they did, yeah.
Not, not right away, but later on, when we all were required to have an ID card with a picture and a fingerprint on that, then they put on the ID cards, with the Jewish, a black J.
So anytime they was  in control, they could, right away, see that man is a Jew.
And then, of course, they picked up some Jews.
It was something around sabotage or whatever they are.
That's the Jews' act.
And then they picked up some Jewish men in, in, in Maastricht or in other places, and sent them to camp, yeah.
I remember one case, one man was, for a reason, arrested by the Germans.
And he was-- they brought him a  transport to Amsterdam.
I knew his wife.
And she was still at  home.
She wasn't-- it was non-Jewish.
And she asked me to, to go along with her in the train.
And so  she talked to me.
And I talked to her.
And what we said was meant for him.
He was sitting in some-- somewhere in the same area with some soldiers who were transferred into Amsterdam.
No, never.
Never.
Yes, one particular friend, by the name Van Assen, V-A-N A-S-S-E-N.
He was arrested, and he had the two Jewish persons in his home.
And they were arrested as well.
They all went-- sent to a camp, and they never came back.
The majority did, yes.
For example, we were not allowed to have a radio.
I have a clandestine radio.
And we listened to the-- to the British broadcast to hear what was going on in the war, you know, and--
That was done secretly, yeah.
We had that hidden somewhere in the-- in the floor.
And at night, we took it out.
And then we listened to the BBC.
Not at that time.
Later on, of course, and then I was aware of that.
By the Germans, yes.
When you informed for the Germans, they, they, they got, of course, their salary and benefits.
Oh, yes.
That's right, yes.
Only, in my, my particular case, it was, [? do you know, ?] a couple of-- three guys, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and another guy did, Theo [NON-ENGLISH], who were informers for the German, yeah.
Yeah?
OK.
